content:
# Acts Chapter 15 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 243-244 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_015_page_001.jpg, ACT_015_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And certain men came down from Judea and were teaching the brothers, "Unless you are circumcised according to the custom of Moses, you cannot be saved."

**2** And when no small dissension and debate arose between Paul and Barnabas and them, they appointed Paul and Barnabas and certain others of them to go up to Jerusalem, to the apostles and elders, concerning this question.

**3** Therefore, having been sent on their way by the church, they passed through both Phoenicia and Samaria, reporting the conversion of the Gentiles, and they caused great joy to all the brothers.

**4** And when they arrived in Jerusalem, they were received by the church and the apostles and the elders, and they reported all that God had done with them.

**5** But some who had believed from the sect of the Pharisees rose up, saying, "It is necessary to circumcise them and to command them to keep the law of Moses."

**6** And the apostles and the elders gathered together to consider this matter.

**7** And when there had been much debate, Peter stood up and said to them, "Brothers, you know that from the early days God chose among you that through my mouth the Gentiles should hear the word of the gospel and believe.

**8** And God, who knows the heart, bore witness to them, giving them the Holy Spirit just as he also did to us,

**9** and he made no distinction between us and them, having cleansed their hearts by faith.

**10** Now therefore, why do you test God, to place a yoke upon the neck of the disciples which neither our fathers nor we were able to bear?

**11** But through the grace of the Lord Jesus we believe that we are saved, in the same way as they also."

**12** And the whole multitude fell silent, and they listened to Barnabas and Paul recounting what signs and wonders God had done among the Gentiles through them.

**13** And after they fell silent, James answered, saying, "Brothers, hear me.

**14** Symeon has related how God first visited to take from the Gentiles a people for his name.

**15** And with this the words of the prophets agree, just as it is written:

**16** *'After these things I will return, and I will rebuild the tent of David which has fallen, and I will rebuild its ruins, and I will restore it,*

**17** *so that the rest of mankind may seek the Lord, and all the Gentiles upon whom my name has been called, says the Lord, who does these things*

**18** *known from of old.'*

**19** Therefore I judge that we should not trouble those from the Gentiles who are turning to God,

**20** but that we should write to them to abstain from the pollutions of idols, and from sexual immorality, and from what is strangled, and from blood.

**21** For Moses from ancient generations has in every city those who preach him, being read in the synagogues every Sabbath."

**22** Then it seemed good to the apostles and the elders, with the whole church, to choose men from among them and send them to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas: Judas called Barsabbas, and Silas, leading men among the brothers,

**23** having written by their hand: "The apostles and the elders, brothers, to the brothers among the Gentiles throughout Antioch and Syria and Cilicia: Greetings.

**24** Since we have heard that certain ones who went out from us have troubled you with words, unsettling your souls -- to whom we gave no instructions --

**25** it seemed good to us, having come to one accord, to choose men and send them to you with our beloved Barnabas and Paul,

**26** men who have given over their lives for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ.

**27** We have therefore sent Judas and Silas, who themselves will also report the same things by word of mouth.

**28** For it seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us to lay upon you no greater burden than these necessary things:

**29** to abstain from things sacrificed to idols, and from blood, and from things strangled, and from sexual immorality; from which, if you keep yourselves, you will do well. Farewell."

**30** So then, having been sent off, they went down to Antioch, and having gathered the multitude together, they delivered the letter.

**31** And when they read it, they rejoiced at the encouragement.

**32** And Judas and Silas, being themselves also prophets, encouraged the brothers with many words and strengthened them.

**33** And after they had spent some time, they were sent away in peace from the brothers to those who had sent them.

**34** [This verse is absent from Codex Sinaiticus.]

**35** But Paul and Barnabas remained in Antioch, teaching and proclaiming the word of the Lord, with many others also.

**36** And after some days, Paul said to Barnabas, "Let us return now and visit the brothers in every city in which we proclaimed the word of the Lord, and see how they are doing."

**37** And Barnabas wished to take along also John, who was called Mark.

**38** But Paul kept insisting that they should not take with them this one who had withdrawn from them in Pamphylia and had not gone with them to the work.

**39** And there arose a sharp contention, so that they separated from one another. And Barnabas, taking Mark, sailed away to Cyprus.

**40** But Paul, having chosen Silas, went out, having been commended by the brothers to the grace of the Lord.

**41** And he traveled through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the churches.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Acts 15 records the most consequential deliberation in the early church: the Jerusalem Council. The question before the assembly -- whether Gentile believers must submit to circumcision and the Mosaic law in order to be saved -- determined the trajectory of the entire Christian movement. The chapter divides into four sections: the crisis in Antioch (vv. 1-5), the Jerusalem debate with speeches by Peter and James (vv. 6-21), the apostolic letter and its joyful reception (vv. 22-35), and the sharp separation of Paul and Barnabas (vv. 36-41). Luke's narrative achieves a striking structural irony: the chapter that resolves the church's greatest theological crisis concludes with its two leading missionaries unable to resolve a dispute over personnel.

**Manuscript condition:** The text of Acts 15 begins in the fourth column of page 243 (image 001), visible at the bottom right of the page with the section marker at Acts 15:2. It continues across all four columns of page 244 (image 002), with the transition from Acts 15 into Acts 16 visible toward the end of the fourth column. The text is well-preserved throughout. Standard nomina sacra are employed: KY/KC (Lord), IY/IC (Jesus), XY/XC (Christ), THC (God), PNEYMA (Holy Spirit abbreviated). The absence of verse 34 is confirmed -- no lacuna or erasure is visible; the text simply proceeds from v. 33 to v. 35. The fourfold decree (idols, blood, strangled, sexual immorality) is clearly legible in both its occurrences (v. 20 and v. 29).

**Agreement among translators:** The three translations show strong agreement on the overall narrative arc and theological content of this chapter. All three correctly identify the Sinaiticus readings: the Hebraized form "Symeon" (v. 14), the fourfold Apostolic Decree (vv. 20, 29), and the absence of verse 34. The primary points of divergence involve: (1) the rendering of porneia (vv. 20, 29), (2) the degree of dynamism in rendering Peter's speech (vv. 7-11), (3) the treatment of the Amos quotation (vv. 16-18), (4) the rendering of paroxysmos (v. 39), and (5) the name form "Symeon" vs. "Simon" (v. 14).

**General translation approach:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS most closely in this chapter, as the formal equivalence approach best preserves both the deliberative speeches (Peter's and James's arguments depend on precise wording) and the embedded documentary text of the apostolic letter (vv. 23-29). EMET's literal renderings are consulted for morphological precision, particularly in the participial constructions and the OT quotation. PNEUMA's dynamic readings inform the consensus in passages where clarity strengthens impact without sacrificing fidelity.

**Key consensus decisions:**

1. **Verse 2 -- "no small dissension" (staseos kai zeteseos ouk oliges):** All three translators preserve the Lukan litotes. EMET renders the full construction literally; LOGOS renders it formally; PNEUMA renders it dynamically as "fierce dispute." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "no small dissension and debate" preserves Luke's characteristic understatement by negation while reading naturally in English. PNEUMA's "fierce dispute" resolves the litotes and loses the stylistic marker.

2. **Verse 11 -- The reversed comparison:** Peter's argument in v. 11 is remarkable for its reversal of the expected direction. He does not say "they are saved like us" but rather "we believe that we are saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, in the same way as they also." This places Jewish believers in the position of needing the same grace extended to Gentiles. All three translators capture this reversal. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's phrasing, which preserves the Greek word order and emphasis most faithfully. EMET's compressed "according to which manner also those" is difficult in English. PNEUMA's exclamatory "No!" is vivid but adds to the text.

3. **Verse 12 -- "Barnabas and Paul" name order:** Sinaiticus preserves the order "Barnabas and Paul" here, reflecting the Jerusalem perspective where Barnabas was the better-known figure. All three translators correctly follow this order. SOPHIA preserves it, noting the contrast with v. 2 ("Paul and Barnabas"), which reflects the Antiochene perspective.

4. **Verse 14 -- "Symeon" (Sumeon):** EMET and LOGOS correctly render the Hebraized form "Symeon" as it appears in Sinaiticus. PNEUMA renders "Simon." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "Symeon" is the form James uses, consistent with his Hebraic speech register at the Council. See the Disagreement Log for full discussion.

5. **Verses 16-18 -- The Amos 9:11-12 quotation:** The LXX text quoted by James differs substantially from the Hebrew MT. All three translators follow the LXX form as preserved in Sinaiticus. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's rendering, which balances fidelity to the Greek with readability. The key phrase "the rest of mankind may seek the Lord" (LXX) vs. "possess the remnant of Edom" (MT) is correctly rendered by all three. SOPHIA renders "mankind" rather than EMET's literal "men" (anthropon) for inclusive clarity, and rather than PNEUMA's "humanity" which shifts register.

6. **Verse 19 -- "I judge" (krino):** James uses the authoritative krino, carrying the weight of a binding ruling. EMET and LOGOS render "I judge"; PNEUMA renders "here is my judgment." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "I judge" preserves the first-person verb and its judicial weight. PNEUMA's periphrasis loses the directness.

7. **Verse 20 -- porneia:** EMET renders "fornication"; LOGOS renders "sexual immorality"; PNEUMA renders "sexual immorality." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1): "sexual immorality" -- the Greek porneia is broader than "fornication" (which in modern English implies specifically premarital intercourse) and encompasses all forms of illicit sexual conduct. See the Disagreement Log for full discussion.

8. **Verse 20 -- "pollutions of idols" (alisgemata ton eidolon):** EMET and LOGOS render "pollutions of idols"; PNEUMA renders "things polluted by idols." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "pollutions of idols" preserves the formal language of ritual contamination. PNEUMA's rendering clarifies the meaning but loses the cultic resonance of alisgemata.

9. **Verse 23 -- Letter salutation:** The greeting formula chairein ("Greetings") matches the standard secular Greek letter opening, identical to the form in James 1:1. All three translators render it appropriately. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "Greetings" with a capital G, as it opens the formal letter.

10. **Verse 24 -- "unsettling your souls" (anaskeuazontes tas psychas hymon):** EMET renders "unsettling the souls of you"; LOGOS renders "unsettling your souls"; PNEUMA renders "unsettled your minds." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "unsettling your souls" preserves psychas ("souls") rather than PNEUMA's "minds," since the Greek refers to the whole inner person, not merely the intellect.

11. **Verse 26 -- "given over their lives" (paradokasin tas psychas auton):** EMET renders "having-handed-over the souls of-them"; LOGOS renders "have given over their lives"; PNEUMA renders "have risked their lives." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "have given over their lives" renders psychas as "lives" (appropriate in this context of mortal risk) while preserving the force of paradidomi. PNEUMA's "risked" is slightly weaker than the Greek, which implies actual handing over, not merely risking.

12. **Verse 28 -- "it seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us":** This extraordinary claim of divine-human collaboration is rendered faithfully by all three translators. SOPHIA preserves the phrasing exactly: the council claimed not direct revelation but Spirit-guided consensus.

13. **Verse 29 -- Fourfold decree:** Sinaiticus presents four prohibitions: things sacrificed to idols (eidolothyton), blood (haimatos), things strangled (pniktou), and sexual immorality (porneias). All three translators correctly include all four, following the Alexandrian text against the Western text (Codex Bezae), which omits "strangled" and adds a negative Golden Rule. SOPHIA preserves the fourfold form.

14. **Verse 29 -- "Farewell" (errōsthe):** EMET renders "Be-strong" (preserving the literal meaning of the imperative); LOGOS and PNEUMA render "Farewell." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1): "Farewell" -- the imperative errōsthe had become a formulaic letter closing meaning "farewell/be well," parallel to the opening chairein. EMET's literal rendering, while etymologically precise, would mislead modern readers into thinking the letter ends with an exhortation rather than a conventional closing.

15. **Verse 34 -- Absence from Sinaiticus:** All three translators correctly note the absence of this verse. The later addition ("But it seemed good to Silas to remain there") was a scribal harmonization to explain Silas's availability in v. 40. SOPHIA marks the verse as absent from the manuscript.

16. **Verse 35 -- "proclaiming the word of the Lord":** EMET renders "proclaiming-good-news the word of the Lord"; LOGOS renders "proclaiming the gospel of the word of the Lord"; PNEUMA renders "proclaiming the word of the Lord." The Greek reads euangelizomenoi ton logon tou kyriou. SOPHIA follows the consensus: "proclaiming the word of the Lord" -- the verb euangelizomai takes ton logon as its direct object. LOGOS's "the gospel of the word" creates a double-noun construction not in the Greek.

17. **Verse 38 -- Paul's objection regarding Mark:** EMET renders "was-considering-worthy...not to-take-along"; LOGOS renders "kept insisting that they should not take with them"; PNEUMA renders "did not think it wise to take him." The Greek axioun carries the sense of deeming worthy or fitting. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "kept insisting" captures the imperfect tense (repeated or sustained action) and the force of Paul's position. PNEUMA's "did not think it wise" softens the verb. EMET's literal rendering is grammatically awkward in English.

18. **Verse 39 -- paroxysmos (sharp contention):** EMET renders "sharp-contention"; LOGOS renders "sharp contention"; PNEUMA renders the whole clause dynamically as "the disagreement between them became so sharp." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "sharp contention" -- the Greek paroxysmos denotes a violent eruption of feeling (whence English "paroxysm"). Luke does not euphemize the rupture between Paul and Barnabas. See the Disagreement Log for full discussion.

19. **Verse 40 -- "commended" (paradotheis):** EMET renders "having-been-handed-over"; LOGOS renders "having been commended"; PNEUMA renders "commended." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "commended" -- the passive participle implies the church's formal endorsement of Paul's mission, in contrast to Barnabas's departure without such note. This subtle Lukan detail may signal the narrative's alignment with Paul's position in the dispute.

20. **Verse 41 -- "churches" (ekklesias):** All three translators render ekklesias as "churches" (EMET: "assemblies"). SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "churches" is the standard English rendering and is more recognizable to readers than EMET's "assemblies," though both are defensible.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 2 | "no small dissension" vs. "fierce dispute" | "no small dissension and debate" (litotes) | "no small dissension and debate" | "fierce dispute" (resolved litotes) | "no small dissension and debate" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; preserves Lukan litotes |
| 2 | "by Paul and Barnabas with them" vs. "against them" | "by-Paul and Barnabas with them" | "by Paul and Barnabas against them" | "Paul and Barnabas clashed sharply with them" | "between Paul and Barnabas and them" -- pros + acc. = toward/against; "between...and" captures the mutual opposition |
| 7 | "Men, brothers" vs. "Brothers" | "Men, brothers" (andres adelphoi) | "Brothers" | "Brothers" | "Brothers" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1); andres adelphoi is a formulaic address; "Brothers" suffices in English |
| 8 | "the heart-knowing God" vs. "God, who knows the heart" | "the heart-knowing God" (kardiognostes) | "God, who knows the heart" | "God, who knows every human heart" | "God, who knows the heart" -- follows LOGOS; relative clause is natural English; PNEUMA's "every human heart" adds to the text |
| 11 | "we-believe to-be-saved" vs. "we believe that we are saved" vs. "We believe that we are saved" | compressed: "we-believe to-be-saved according-to which manner" | "we believe that we are saved, in the same way as they also" | "We believe that we are saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, and so are they" | "we believe that we are saved, in the same way as they also" -- follows LOGOS; preserves the reversed comparison |
| 12 | "became-silent" vs. "fell silent" | "became-silent" (esigesen) | "fell silent" | "fell silent" | "fell silent" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1); more vivid English |
| **14** | **"Symeon" vs. "Simon"** | "Symeon" (Sumeon) | "Symeon" | "Simon" | **"Symeon"** -- follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1); Hebraized form in James's speech; see detailed discussion in disagreements file |
| 16 | "tent" vs. "tent" vs. "tent" | "tent" (skenen) | "tent" | "tent" | "tent" -- unanimous; skene = tent/tabernacle/dwelling |
| 16 | "having-been-dug-down of-it I-shall-build-again" vs. "I will rebuild its ruins" vs. "I will restore its ruins" | "the-[things] having-been-dug-down of-it I-shall-build-again" | "I will rebuild its ruins" | "I will restore its ruins" | "I will rebuild its ruins" -- follows LOGOS; ta kateskammena = "the things torn down" rendered as "ruins" |
| 17 | "the rest of-the men" vs. "the rest of mankind" vs. "the rest of humanity" | "the rest of-the men" (hoi kataloipoi ton anthropon) | "the rest of mankind" | "the rest of humanity" | "the rest of mankind" -- follows LOGOS; anthropon rendered inclusively |
| 18 | "known from [the] age" vs. "known from of old" vs. "known from long ago" | "known from [the] age" (gnosta ap aionos) | "known from of old" | "known from long ago" | "known from of old" -- follows LOGOS; ap aionos = from the age/eternity; "of old" preserves the archaic prophetic register |
| 19 | "I judge not to-trouble" vs. "I judge that we should not trouble" vs. "we should stop making it difficult" | "I judge not to-trouble" | "I judge that we should not trouble" | "here is my judgment: we should stop making it difficult" | "I judge that we should not trouble" -- follows LOGOS; preserves krino as verb; PNEUMA's "stop making it difficult" is interpretive |
| **20** | **porneia: "fornication" vs. "sexual immorality"** | "fornication" | "sexual immorality" | "sexual immorality" | **"sexual immorality"** -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1); porneia is broader than modern "fornication"; see detailed discussion in disagreements file |
| 20 | "pollutions of-the idols" vs. "pollutions of idols" vs. "things polluted by idols" | "pollutions of-the idols" | "pollutions of idols" | "things polluted by idols" | "pollutions of idols" -- follows LOGOS; preserves alisgemata |
| 21 | "from generations of-old" vs. "from ancient generations" vs. "for generations" | "from generations of-old" | "from ancient generations" | "for generations" | "from ancient generations" -- follows LOGOS; ek geneon archaion |
| 23 | Letter address structure | "to-the-[ones] throughout Antioch...brothers from the Gentiles" | "to the brothers throughout Antioch...who are from the Gentiles" | "to the Gentile believers in Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia" | "to the brothers among the Gentiles throughout Antioch and Syria and Cilicia" -- composite; preserves "brothers" as addressees and "Gentiles" as their identity |
| 24 | "to-whom we-gave no-instruction" vs. "to whom we gave no instructions" vs. "without any authorization from us" | "to-whom we-gave no-instruction" | "to whom we gave no instructions" | "without any authorization from us" | "to whom we gave no instructions" -- follows LOGOS; relative clause preserves Greek syntax; PNEUMA's "without any authorization" is interpretive |
| 26 | "having-handed-over the souls" vs. "have given over their lives" vs. "have risked their lives" | "having-handed-over the souls of-them" | "have given over their lives" | "have risked their lives" | "have given over their lives" -- follows LOGOS; paradidomi = hand over/give over; "risked" (PNEUMA) is weaker |
| 29 | "Be-strong" vs. "Farewell" vs. "Farewell" | "Be-strong" (errōsthe, literal) | "Farewell" | "Farewell" | "Farewell" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1); formulaic letter closing |
| **34** | **Verse absent from Sinaiticus** | "[This verse is absent in Sinaiticus.]" | "[omitted]" | "[This verse is absent from Sinaiticus.]" | **"[This verse is absent from Codex Sinaiticus.]"** -- follows EMET/PNEUMA format with full manuscript name; see detailed discussion in disagreements file |
| 35 | "proclaiming-good-news the word" vs. "proclaiming the gospel of the word" vs. "proclaiming the word" | "proclaiming-good-news the word of-the Lord" | "proclaiming the gospel of the word of the Lord" | "proclaiming the word of the Lord" | "proclaiming the word of the Lord" -- follows PNEUMA; euangelizomenoi ton logon: the verb takes the direct object |
| 36 | "how they-are-having" vs. "how they are doing" vs. "how they are doing" | "how they-are-having" (pos echousin) | "how they are doing" | "how they are doing" | "how they are doing" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1); natural English idiom |
| 38 | "was-considering-worthy...not to-take-along" vs. "kept insisting" vs. "did not think it wise" | "was-considering-worthy...not to-take-along" (axioun) | "kept insisting that they should not take" | "did not think it wise to take" | "kept insisting" -- follows LOGOS; captures imperfect tense and force of axioun |
| **39** | **paroxysmos rendering** | "sharp-contention" | "sharp contention" | "disagreement...became so sharp" | **"sharp contention"** -- follows LOGOS; preserves paroxysmos as noun; see detailed discussion in disagreements file |
| 40 | "having-been-handed-over" vs. "having been commended" vs. "commended" | "having-been-handed-over" (paradotheis) | "having been commended" | "commended" | "having been commended" -- follows LOGOS; paradidomi in ecclesial context = commend/entrust |
| 41 | "assemblies" vs. "churches" | "assemblies" (ekklesias) | "churches" | "churches" | "churches" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1); standard English rendering of ekklesia |

-- SOPHIA
